The **1999–2000 season** was the 120th season of competitive [football in England](/wiki/Football_in_England "Football in England").

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

### Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Premier League")]

[Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") were crowned [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") champions with an 18-point margin over runners-up [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") and with just 3 league defeats all season. This was despite their failure to retain the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and withdrawal from the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") in order to compete in the [FIFA Club World Championship](/wiki/FIFA_Club_World_Championship "FIFA Club World Championship") – a campaign which was short lived. [Andy Townsend](/wiki/Andy_Townsend "Andy Townsend") also once said in *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")* that Manchester United should be "banned for life" from the FA Cup. Their season after the domination of 1998–99 was seen as a relative failure by the tabloids. Man United's failure in the FIFA Club World Championship, was surprisingly compounded by the press even more when [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham")'s wife [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_Beckham "Victoria Beckham"), admitted on *[The Big Breakfast](/wiki/The_Big_Breakfast "The Big Breakfast")* that he enjoys wearing her [thongs](/wiki/Thong "Thong"). It was during this tournament he was [sent off](/wiki/Penalty_card "Penalty card") against Mexican team [Club Necaxa](/wiki/Club_Necaxa "Club Necaxa"), which was seen as the starting point in his team's first-round exit.

[Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") made an impressive [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") comeback by finishing seventh and just missing out on a [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") place, in part due to 30 goals from prolific striker [Kevin Phillips](/wiki/Kevin_Phillips_(footballer) "Kevin Phillips (footballer)").

[Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") went down in bottom place with a record [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") low of 24 points. [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") were next to go down. On the last day of the season, [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") lost 2–0 at [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") and [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") achieved a shock 1–0 home win over [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") thanks to a goal by [David Wetherall](/wiki/David_Wetherall "David Wetherall"). This meant that Bradford had survived the drop and Liverpool had surrendered a [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") place to [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_A.F.C. "Leeds United A.F.C."). It also meant that Wimbledon's 14-year spell in the top flight was over and they were relegated to Division One 12 years to the day that they had beaten Liverpool to achieve a shock [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") triumph.

### Division One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Division One")]

The three promotion places in [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_Division_One "Football League Division One") went to league champions [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C."), runners-up [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") and playoff champions [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.").

1999–2000 also saw some of Division One's biggest clubs miss out on promotion—the biggest of these were [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") (11th) and [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") (14th). [Steve Coppell](/wiki/Steve_Coppell "Steve Coppell") ended his fourth spell as [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") manager after doing wonders to keep a virtually bankrupt club clear of the Division One relegation zone.

Going down were [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C."), [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.") and [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C."). [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") just missed out on the drop zone thanks to a late turn-around in form during the final weeks of the season which followed the appointment of [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson") as manager.

### Division Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Division Two")]

[David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes"), 37, showed promise as one of the league's most highly rated young managers after he guided [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") to the [Division Two](/wiki/Football_League_Division_Two "Football League Division Two") championship. [Stan Ternent](/wiki/Stan_Ternent "Stan Ternent")'s two-year rebuilding project at [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") paid off as they finished runners-up in the division. Joining them in Division One were [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer born 1953)")'s [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C."), who had reached the upper half of the league for the first time in their history.

Going down were [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C."), [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") and [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C."). Narrowly avoiding the drop were [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C."), who struggled all season long despite the club's financial crisis being eased by the arrival of new [Tanzanian](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") chairman [Firoz Kassam](/wiki/Firoz_Kassam "Firoz Kassam").

### Division Three

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Division Three")]

[Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."), [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C."), [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.") and [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C.") occupied the four promotion places in [Division Three](/wiki/Football_League_Division_Three "Football League Division Three").

[Chester City](/wiki/Chester_City_F.C. "Chester City F.C.") were relegated on the last day of the season, ending their 69-year league career, while [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C.") and [Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C.") saved themselves from Conference football.

### FA Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: FA Cup")]

[Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") beat [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") 1–0 in the last [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") final at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") before its redevelopment. The competition was played despite the absence of holders [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United") due to [FIFA Club World Championship](/wiki/FIFA_Club_World_Cup "FIFA Club World Cup") commitments. Their absence meant that in the 3rd round of the competition, there was an uneven number of teams, and so [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.") were picked out of a hat of "lucky losers" and played against Aston Villa despite having lost in the 2nd round of the competition. Villa beat them 2–1.

In a one-off season, most FA Cup rounds were played a few weeks earlier than their traditional dates. For example, the first round was played in late October (rather than mid-November) and the third round was held on the second Saturday in December (instead of its annual place at the start of the new year). The experiment has not been repeated since.

### League Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: League Cup")]

[Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") beat [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers_F.C. "Tranmere Rovers F.C.") 2–1 to yield their second [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") in four seasons.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Events")]

### United retain their title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: United retain their title")]

Manchester United retained their Premiership title after losing just three league games all season and finishing 18 points ahead of runners-up Arsenal. Roy Keane was voted Player of the Year by both the PFA and FWA and Dwight Yorke finished as the club's top scorer with 20 Premiership goals. United lost their defence of the European Cup after losing to eventual winners Real Madrid in the quarter-finals. Along the way they suffered an embarrassing 5–0 domestic loss to Chelsea, the biggest ever defeat for a British team defending the European Cup.

### Chelsea triumph in Wembley finale

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Chelsea triumph in Wembley finale")]

Chelsea beat Aston Villa 1–0 in the last FA Cup final at Wembley before the stadium was closed for reconstruction. Robert di Matteo, who had scored in the 1997 final, scored the only goal of the 2000 final to give Chelsea their third FA Cup triumph and end Villa's hopes of winning the trophy for the first time since 1957.

### O'Neill lured to Celtic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: O'Neill lured to Celtic")]

Martin O'Neill guided Leicester City to their second League Cup triumph in four seasons before moving north of the border to Celtic, whose 'dream team' partnership of Director of Football Kenny Dalglish and Head Coach John Barnes had ended a nightmare as they finished 21 points behind champions Rangers in the SPL title race and had suffered a shock SFA Cup exit at the hands of Inverness Caledonian Thistle, which had inspired *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")* newspaper headline: "[Super Caley Go Ballistic, Celtic Are Atrocious](/wiki/Super_Caley_go_ballistic,_Celtic_are_atrocious "Super Caley go ballistic, Celtic are atrocious")".

### England blow it at Euro 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: England blow it at Euro 2000")]

England overcame Scotland in the qualification playoffs to qualify for Euro 2000, and beat Germany in the group stages. But they surrendered a two-goal lead to lose 3–2 to Portugal and lost by the same scoreline to Romania, and thus failed to reach the quarter-finals. This dismal showing saw continued calls for the F.A. to terminate Kevin Keegan's contract.

### Veteran Robson takes over on Tyneside

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Veteran Robson takes over on Tyneside")]

Bobby Robson, 66, became the oldest manager ever to take charge of a Premiership club when he succeeded 37-year-old Ruud Gullit as manager of Newcastle United.

### Chester out, Kidderminster in

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Chester out, Kidderminster in")]

Chester City were relegated to the Conference after 69 years of Football League membership. They were replaced by Conference champions Kidderminster Harriers, who had won the Conference title six years earlier but had been refused promotion because their Aggborough ground did not meet capacity requirements.

### Coppell quits Palace again

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Coppell quits Palace again")]

Steve Coppell was sacked as Crystal Palace manager, having been in charge of the club four times in 16 years. New owner Simon Jordan brought in Alan Smith (who had managed Palace between 1993 and 1995) as Coppell's successor. The change of hands gave Palace fans hope for the future after a financial crisis had almost put the club out of business.

### Shearer quits the international stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Shearer quits the international stage")]

Alan Shearer retired from international football after playing for England at Euro 2000, much to the dismay of many fans who felt that he still had a few years left in him at the highest level.

### Wimbledon relegated after 14 years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Wimbledon relegated after 14 years")]

Wimbledon were relegated from the Premiership after 14 years in the top flight. Their fate was sealed on 14 May, 12 years to the day since they had achieved their famous FA Cup victory over Liverpool.

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Awards")]

[Roy Keane](/wiki/Roy_Keane "Roy Keane") captained [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") to another [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") title triumph and was credited with the [PFA Players' Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Players%27_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Players' Player of the Year") and [FWA Footballer of the Year](/wiki/FWA_Footballer_of_the_Year "FWA Footballer of the Year") awards.

[Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.")'s 22-year-old Australian midfielder [Harry Kewell](/wiki/Harry_Kewell "Harry Kewell") was voted [PFA Young Player of the Year](/wiki/PFA_Young_Player_of_the_Year "PFA Young Player of the Year") after helping to secure a Champions League place for his side.

[Kevin Phillips](/wiki/Kevin_Phillips_(footballer) "Kevin Phillips (footballer)") scored 30 [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") goals for [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") to win the [Premier League Golden Boot](/wiki/Premier_League_Golden_Boot "Premier League Golden Boot") and making him the season's highest scorer among all four English professional divisions.

## Successful managers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Successful managers")]

[Sir Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson") guided [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") to their sixth [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") title in eight years.

[Gianluca Vialli](/wiki/Gianluca_Vialli "Gianluca Vialli") won his fourth major trophy in two-and-a-half seasons by winning the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") for [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.").

[David O'Leary](/wiki/David_O%27Leary "David O'Leary") ended his first full season as [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") manager with a run to the semi-finals of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") and qualification for next season's Champions League.

[Peter Reid](/wiki/Peter_Reid "Peter Reid") achieved a remarkable seventh-place finish for [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") in their [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") comeback season.

[Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill") ended his spell at [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") by achieving the Foxes' highest ever Premier League finish and by winning a second [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") in four seasons.

[Joe Royle](/wiki/Joe_Royle "Joe Royle") took [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") into the [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") after winning a second successive promotion.

[Alan Curbishley](/wiki/Alan_Curbishley "Alan Curbishley") guided Charlton Athletic to promotion back to the Premiership at the first attempt after they finished champions of Division One.

[George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley") finally got Ipswich Town into the Premiership when they triumphed in the Division One playoffs after three successive seasons of failure.

[David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes") continued Preston North End's revival by guiding them to title success in Division Two.

[Stan Ternent](/wiki/Stan_Ternent "Stan Ternent") guided Burnley to runners-up spot in Division Two and secured their promotion to Division One.

[John Hollins](/wiki/John_Hollins "John Hollins") brought success to Swansea City, who won the Division Three title.

## League tables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: League tables")]

### FA Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: FA Premier League")]

Main article: [1999–2000 FA Premier League](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_FA_Premier_League "1999–2000 FA Premier League")

Manchester United retained the Premier League title, winning it for the sixth time in eight seasons, and finished the season as champions by a record 18-point margin; while their season was marred by faltering results in the early stages, a struggle to replace long-serving goalkeeper [Peter Schmeichel](/wiki/Peter_Schmeichel "Peter Schmeichel"), and their controversially withdrawing from the FA Cup in favour of an ultimately fruitless entry into the [2000 FIFA Club World Championship](/wiki/2000_FIFA_Club_World_Championship "2000 FIFA Club World Championship"), they made up for this in style by winning all but three games (only one of which ended in defeat) after Christmas.

Arsenal finished runners-up for the second successive season, having spent much of the season well off the pace of the Champions League spots before a strong run in the late stages saw them clinch second place. Leeds United's season was essentially the opposite of those of the two teams above them, leading the table for much of the first half of the season, only for their form to completely implode in the final months, with only Liverpool's failure to win on the final day securing them a Champions League spot; their third-place finish was nonetheless their highest since title glory in 1992.

Liverpool's fourth-place finish meant that they had to settle for a UEFA Cup place, a shock defeat to Bradford City (who defied all the odds to avoid relegation in their first top-flight season for nearly 80 years) preventing them from qualifying for the Champions League on the final day. Joining Liverpool in the UEFA Cup were fifth placed FA Cup Winners Chelsea, and League Cup winners Leicester City. Newly promoted Sunderland just missed out on Europe, with a winless run from Boxing Day to mid-March ending their hopes, but their seventh-place finish was their highest for decades, and star striker Kevin Phillips bagged both the Golden Boot and the European Golden shoe, scoring 30 goals.

[Ruud Gullit](/wiki/Ruud_Gullit "Ruud Gullit") stepped down as Newcastle manager after one point from the first five games of the league campaign, paving the way for 66-year-old former England manager [Bobby Robson](/wiki/Bobby_Robson "Bobby Robson") to take up his first post in England since leaving the England job in 1990, and his first at club level since 1982. Robson steered Newcastle to an 11th-place finish and they also reached the FA Cup semi-finals.

Bradford's escape from relegation meant that Wimbledon, who were beaten by Southampton on the final day of the season, were relegated from the Premiership after 14 successive seasons of top-flight football. Sheffield Wednesday went down after three seasons of steady decline, having been in the top flight for all but one season since 1984, as a horrific first half of the season, which saw them bottom with a paltry 6 points at Christmas, ultimately proved too much to recover from. Watford's first top-flight season for more than a decade ended in relegation, being undone by a poor run of form from late September onwards which saw them slip into the bottom three, where they couldn't recover, ultimately resulting in them posting the lowest-ever points total for a Premier League team until that point.

### Football League Division One

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Football League Division One")]

Main article: [1999–2000 Football League § First Division](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League#First_Division "1999–2000 Football League")

Charlton Athletic won the division with greater ease than the final table suggested – they were in fact the first team in the country to win promotion, but a return of just 4 points from their last 7 matches allowed the pursuing teams to close the gap. Manchester City won their second successive promotion in the runners-up spot, well and truly putting the memories of their relegation to the Second Division behind them. Ipswich Town finished in the play-offs for the fourth season in a row, but this time they were successful as they triumphed over Barnsley at Wembley and won promotion back to the Premier League after a five-year absence.

Wolves narrowly missed out on the playoffs for the third season running, while Huddersfield Town's eighth-place finish was perhaps the closest they had come to returning to the top flight since losing their top-flight place in 1972. Fulham finished ninth as their recent revival continued, but a failure to win a second successive promotion saw [Paul Bracewell](/wiki/Paul_Bracewell "Paul Bracewell") sacked as manager and replaced with [Jean Tigana](/wiki/Jean_Tigana "Jean Tigana"), who had won a French league title and reached a European Cup semi-final with [AS Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco "AS Monaco").

Swindon suffered from a financial crisis and a transfer embargo for most of the season, and the effect on the pitch was obvious as they were relegated in bottom place. Port Vale, who had only survived the previous season due to the League's short-lived usage of Goals Scored over Goal Difference, went down with them. Walsall took their survival battle to the final day of the season, but were unable to avoid an immediate relegation back to Division Two.

### Football League Division Two

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Football League Division Two")]

Main article: [1999–2000 Football League § Second Division](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League#Second_Division "1999–2000 Football League")

Preston won the division title after 19 years away from the top two divisions, showing great progress under promising young manager [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes"). [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."), who were unfancied and even tipped for relegation by some owing to two unimpressive previous seasons, achieved promotion in the second automatic spot. After losing in the previous year's play-off final, Gillingham won promotion via the Division Two playoffs to reach the upper half of the English league for the first time in their history, their opponents in the play-off final being a [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic "Wigan Athletic") side playing their first season in the impressive new 25,000-seat [JJB Stadium](/wiki/DW_Stadium "DW Stadium").

Chesterfield started the season well enough, but a dreadful run of 2 wins in 20 games mid-season consigned them to relegation and spelled the end as manager for [John Duncan](/wiki/John_Duncan_(footballer) "John Duncan (footballer)") after seven years. Scunthorpe, who had won promotion via the play-offs the previous year, never achieved enough wins to have a serious chance of survival, and went straight back down. Blackpool had been coping well in previous seasons despite financial problems, but they finally took their toll this season and helped send the club down. Cardiff were the final relegated team, eventually proving the weakest of a group of sides that could have easily gone down in the last weeks of the season. The highlight of their season was their game against [Cambridge United](/wiki/Cambridge_United "Cambridge United"), where they drew despite being a player down after 45 minutes, two players down after 63 minutes and 3 players down after 75 minutes. Oxford United finished one point clear of a second successive relegation, their future secured after being taken over by hotelier [Firoz Kassam](/wiki/Firoz_Kassam "Firoz Kassam"), who enabled construction work to resume on the club's long-awaited [new stadium](/wiki/Kassam_Stadium "Kassam Stadium").[[1]](#cite_note-3)

### Football League Division Three

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Football League Division Three")]

Main article: [1999–2000 Football League § Third Division](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League#Third_Division "1999–2000 Football League")

Swansea and Rotherham, both of whom had been dumped out in the play-off semi-finals the previous year, led the table for virtually the entire season. Swansea eventually won the title by drawing a stormy last game of the season between the two sides. The third automatic spot was won by Northampton, who were led to promotion by new manager [Kevin Wilson](/wiki/Kevin_Wilson_(footballer_born_1961) "Kevin Wilson (footballer born 1961)") after a poor start to the season under previous manager [Ian Atkins](/wiki/Ian_Atkins "Ian Atkins"). Peterborough won the play-offs, returning to Division Two after an absence of three years.

A four-way relegation scrap took place throughout most of the season, involving Chester, Shrewsbury, Carlisle and York. York eventually pulled away to safety with some good results in the final weeks of the season, leaving the other three to face a final-day relegation battle. In the end, Chester, who had spent virtually the entire season rooted to the bottom of the table and only had the chance of a last-day escape because of similarly poor campaigns by Carlisle and Shrewsbury, were relegated to the Conference.

## Diary of the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Diary of the season")]

17 June 1999 – [David James](/wiki/David_James_(footballer,_born_1970) "David James (footballer, born 1970)") leaves [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") after seven years in a £1.7 million move to [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.").

21 June 1999 – The [France national team](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") captain [Didier Deschamps](/wiki/Didier_Deschamps "Didier Deschamps") joins [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in a £3 million move from [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC").

30 June 1999 – [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") prepare for their first time top division season for nearly 80 years with a club record £1.4 million move for [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") defender [David Wetherall](/wiki/David_Wetherall "David Wetherall").

5 July 1999 – Chelsea pay a club record £10 million for [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") striker [Chris Sutton](/wiki/Chris_Sutton "Chris Sutton").

14 July 1999 – [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") pay [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") £6.5 million for midfielder [Kieron Dyer](/wiki/Kieron_Dyer "Kieron Dyer") and [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") pay [Trabzonspor](/wiki/Trabzonspor "Trabzonspor") £3 million for on-loan striker [Kevin Campbell](/wiki/Kevin_Campbell_(footballer) "Kevin Campbell (footballer)"), who scored eight goals in seven games for them late last season.

23 July 1999 – Leeds United sign striker [Michael Bridges](/wiki/Michael_Bridges "Michael Bridges") from [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") for £5 million.

29 July 1999 – Four years after leaving [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), [Stefan Schwarz](/wiki/Stefan_Schwarz "Stefan Schwarz") returns to English football in a £4 million move to Sunderland from [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF").

31 July 1999 – [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") sign [German](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") international midfielder [Christian Ziege](/wiki/Christian_Ziege "Christian Ziege") from [AC Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") for £4 million.

1 August 1999 – Arsenal lift the [Charity Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield") thanks to a 2–1 victory over [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)").

2 August 1999 – Arsenal sell [Nicolas Anelka](/wiki/Nicolas_Anelka "Nicolas Anelka") to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") for £23 million – the highest fee involving a British club.

3 August 1999 – Arsenal sign [Thierry Henry](/wiki/Thierry_Henry "Thierry Henry") for an estimated fee of £11 million, reuniting with his former manager [Arsène Wenger](/wiki/Ars%C3%A8ne_Wenger "Arsène Wenger").

4 August 1999 – Leeds United sell [Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink](/wiki/Jimmy_Floyd_Hasselbaink "Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink") to [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") of Spain for £12 million.

7 August 1999 – [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") mark their first game at the [JJB Stadium](/wiki/JJB_Stadium "JJB Stadium") with a 3–0 win over [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") in [Division Two](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division"). The first Premier League games of the season include Chelsea's 4–0 home win over newly-promoted Sunderland and [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.")'s 3–2 home defeat against [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") in their return to the top flight. Bradford City mark their first top-flight game since 1922 with a 1–0 away win over [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C."), in which [Dean Saunders](/wiki/Dean_Saunders "Dean Saunders") scores a late winner.

10 August 1999 – [Kevin Phillips](/wiki/Kevin_Phillips_(footballer) "Kevin Phillips (footballer)") scores his first two Premier League goals in Sunderland's 2–0 home win over his old club Watford.

11 August 1999 – Manchester United beat [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") 4–0 at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"). [Michael Bridges](/wiki/Michael_Bridges "Michael Bridges") scores a hat-trick for Leeds United in their 3–0 away win over Southampton. Leeds United sign [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") striker [Darren Huckerby](/wiki/Darren_Huckerby "Darren Huckerby") for £4.4 million.

14 August 1999 – [Tommy Mooney](/wiki/Tommy_Mooney "Tommy Mooney") scores for Watford in their shock 1–0 league win at Liverpool.

18 August 1999 – 19-year-old [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") striker [Robbie Keane](/wiki/Robbie_Keane "Robbie Keane") becomes Coventry City's record signing in a £6 million deal.

21 August 1999 – Manchester United captain [Roy Keane](/wiki/Roy_Keane "Roy Keane") scores twice in a 2–1 win over Arsenal at [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium").

25 August 1999 – Kevin Phillips and [Niall Quinn](/wiki/Niall_Quinn "Niall Quinn") plunge Newcastle United into crisis as they score in Sunderland's 2–1 derby win at St. James' Park,

27 August 1999 – [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio") beat Manchester United 1–0 in [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco") to win the [European Super Cup](/wiki/European_Super_Cup "European Super Cup").

28 August 1999 – [Ruud Gullit](/wiki/Ruud_Gullit "Ruud Gullit") resigns after one year as manager of Newcastle United.

30 August 1999 – [Andy Cole](/wiki/Andrew_Cole "Andrew Cole") scores four goals against his old club Newcastle United as Manchester United beat them 5–1 at Old Trafford.

31 August 1999 – The first month of the league season ends with treble winners Manchester United looking well placed for retaining their league title as Premier League leaders with five wins and a draw from their first six games. Aston Villa are their nearest contenders, while Chelsea, West Ham United and Leicester City complete the top five. Sheffield Wednesday and Newcastle United, both winless, prop up the rest of the top flight, while Bradford City occupy the final bottom three position at the end of the first month of their first top division season for nearly 80 years.[[3]](#cite_note-5) Ipswich Town are hopeful of making it back to the Premier League after five seasons away (during which time they have suffered three play-off disappointments) as they finish the first month of the season as Division One leaders. [Stockport County](/wiki/Stockport_County_F.C. "Stockport County F.C.") have emerged as surprise contenders for a Premier League place as they stand second in Division One. The playoff zone is occupied by [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-6)

3 September 1999 – Newcastle United appoint [Bobby Robson](/wiki/Bobby_Robson "Bobby Robson") as their new manager. The former [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") manager, 66, is the oldest manager in all four division of the English league – and almost 30 years older than his predecessor Ruud Gullit.

7 September 1999 – [Ade Akinbiyi](/wiki/Ade_Akinbiyi "Ade Akinbiyi") becomes the most expensive Division Two player when he leaves [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City "Bristol City") for Division One promotion hopefuls Wolverhampton Wanderers.

11 September 1999 – [Jamie Carragher](/wiki/Jamie_Carragher "Jamie Carragher") scores two own goals as Liverpool lose 3–2 at home to Manchester United in the league.

17 September 1999 – Former England and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") full-back [Rob Jones](/wiki/Rob_Jones_(footballer,_born_1971) "Rob Jones (footballer, born 1971)") retires from football at the age of 28 after failing to recover from a succession of injuries.

18 September 1999 – Newcastle United record the second highest win in [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") history when they beat Sheffield Wednesday 8–0. [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer") scores five goals in this game, while Kevin Phillips scores a hat-trick in Sunderland's 5–0 win at Derby County. In a rare appearance for Manchester United, [Jordi Cruyff](/wiki/Jordi_Cruyff "Jordi Cruyff") scores a late equaliser in their 1–1 home draw with Wimbledon.

30 September 1999 – Manchester United retain their lead of the Premier League, with Leeds United now their nearest contenders by a two-point margin. Arsenal, Sunderland and Chelsea complete the top five. Sheffield Wednesday remain bottom after a terrible first two months of the season, while Newcastle United remain second from bottom despite that record-breaking victory 12 days ago, and Wimbledon now occupy the final remaining relegation position.[[5]](#cite_note-7) Ipswich Town and Birmingham City are level on points at the top of Division One. [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C."), Manchester City, Fulham and [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") complete the top six.[[6]](#cite_note-8)

2 October 1999 – Sheffield Wednesday record a 5–1 home win over Wimbledon.

3 October 1999 – Chelsea crush Manchester United 5–0 – the first domestic competitive game that United have lost for nearly nine months. [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer") scores twice as Newcastle beat Middlesbrough 2–1.

24 October 1999 – A Premier League thriller at [Goodison Park](/wiki/Goodison_Park "Goodison Park") sees Everton and Leeds United draw 4–4, a result that ends the Yorkshire club's 10-match winning streak. Elsewhere, a late [Trevor Sinclair](/wiki/Trevor_Sinclair "Trevor Sinclair") equalizer for West Ham United stops ten-man Sunderland's ascension to the top; the Black Cats instead rise to third.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

31 October 1999 – October draws to a close with Leeds United as the new Premier League leaders, with Manchester United in second place and trailing them by two points. Arsenal, Sunderland and Leicester City complete the top five. Sheffield Wednesday remain bottom, with Watford and Bradford City completing the bottom three.[[8]](#cite_note-10) Manchester City lead Division One, with Ipswich Town, Charlton Athletic and Birmingham City level on points as their nearest rivals. [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_A.F.C. "Huddersfield Town A.F.C.") and Fulham complete the top six.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

13 November 1999 – [Paul Scholes](/wiki/Paul_Scholes "Paul Scholes") scores twice as [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") beat [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") 2–0 at [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park") in the [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") qualifying playoff first leg.[[10]](#cite_note-englandfootballonline1999-12)

17 November 1999 — England qualify for Euro 2000 despite a 1–0 second leg defeat by Scotland at Wembley.[[10]](#cite_note-englandfootballonline1999-12) Arsenal announce plans to move to a new 60,000-seat stadium at [Ashburton Grove](/wiki/Ashburton_Grove "Ashburton Grove") near [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium"), their home since 1913.

20 November 1999 – [Marc Overmars](/wiki/Marc_Overmars "Marc Overmars") scores a hat-trick in Arsenal's 5–1 home league win over Middlesbrough.

30 November 1999 – Manchester United are back on top of the Premier League as November draws to a close, with former leaders Leeds United now bracketed on points with Arsenal as their nearest rivals. Newly promoted Sunderland's excellent start to the season sees them still in fourth place, with a resurgent Liverpool standing fifth. Sheffield Wednesday and Watford continue to prop up the rest of the Premier League, accompanied in the drop zone by a Derby County side who had spent the previous two seasons chasing a place in Europe.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Manchester City remain top of Division One, with Huddersfield Town now second and in the hunt for top division for the first time in nearly 30 years. Charlton Athletic, Ipswich Town, Barnsley and Fulham occupy the playoff places.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

1 December 1999 – Film producer and actor [Bill Kenwright](/wiki/Bill_Kenwright "Bill Kenwright"), 54, completes a takeover of [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") after buying out former owner [Peter Johnson](/wiki/Peter_Johnson_(businessman) "Peter Johnson (businessman)").

4 December 1999 – [Ole Gunnar Solskjaer](/wiki/Ole_Gunnar_Solskjaer "Ole Gunnar Solskjaer") scores four goals in Manchester United's 5–1 home league win over Everton.

31 December 1999 – 1999 draws to a close and as the new millennium approaches, Leeds United are back on top though with just a one-point lead over Manchester United who have a game in hand. Arsenal, Sunderland and Liverpool are the only three teams still posing a reasonable threat to the top two. Sheffield Wednesday's terrible season has continued as they remain bottom with a mere two wins and nine points from their first 20 games, while Watford and Derby County remain in the bottom three.[[13]](#cite_note-15) The race for promotion to the Premier League is still headed by Manchester City and Huddersfield Town, while Charlton Athletic, Ipswich Town, Barnsley and Stockport County complete the top six.[[14]](#cite_note-16)

8 January 2000 – Liverpool suffer a shock 1–0 home defeat to Blackburn Rovers in the FA Cup fourth round. Tranmere Rovers, on a good run in the League Cup, progress to the fifth round of the FA Cup after a surprise 1–0 win at home to Sunderland in the fourth round. There is a major shock also for Bradford City, who are crushed 3–1 by Division Two Gillingham at [Priestfield](/wiki/Priestfield_Stadium "Priestfield Stadium").

12 January 2000 – Liberian striker [George Weah](/wiki/George_Weah "George Weah"), 31, joins Chelsea on a six-month loan from [A.C. Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan").

19 January 2000 – Arsenal's hopes of FA Cup glory are ended in a replay penalty shoot-out defeat by Leicester City, who boost their own bid for a cup double.

29 January 2000 – The FA Cup fifth round brings some surprise results as Gillingham's giant-killing run continues with a 3–1 home win over Sheffield Wednesday and Charlton Athletic win at Coventry City to give the rest of the Premier League some idea of what they will be performing like when inevitable promotion is achieved. Leicester City's hopes of a domestic cup double are ended when they lose 2–1 at Chelsea, while Cambridge United's impressive run ends in a 3–1 home defeat by Bolton Wanderers. On the transfer front, Division One strugglers West Bromwich Albion sell their Italian midfielder [Enzo Maresca](/wiki/Enzo_Maresca "Enzo Maresca") to [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus") for £4 million.

30 January 2000 – [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") striker [Martin Aldridge](/wiki/Martin_Aldridge_(footballer) "Martin Aldridge (footballer)"), 25, on loan to [Rushden & Diamonds](/wiki/Rushden_%26_Diamonds_F.C. "Rushden & Diamonds F.C."), dies in an [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") hospital after being injured in a car crash in [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire").

31 January 2000 – Struggling Sheffield Wednesday complete the month unbeaten in the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League"). [Danny Wilson](/wiki/Danny_Wilson_(footballer,_born_1960) "Danny Wilson (footballer, born 1960)") is named [FA Premier League Manager of the Month](/wiki/FA_Premier_League_Manager_of_the_Month "FA Premier League Manager of the Month"), but the club are still in the relegation zone, though no longer in the bottom place (now occupied by Watford. Bradford City occupy the other relegation position. Manchester United now lead at the top on goal difference ahead of Leeds United, with Arsenal and Liverpool still posing a threat but Sunderland's challenge is ebbing away.[[15]](#cite_note-17) Charlton Athletic have taken over from Manchester City as Division One leaders. Ipswich Town, Barnsley, Huddersfield Town and Wolverhampton Wanderers complete the top six.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

12 February 2000 – Manchester United suffer their first Premier League defeat in four months, and only their third of the season, as they lose 3–0 at Newcastle United. A Premier League thriller at [Upton Park](/wiki/Boleyn_Ground "Boleyn Ground") sees West Ham United beat Bradford City 5–4.

19 February 2000 – Bolton Wanderers reach the FA Cup semi-finals with a 1–0 win over Charlton Athletic as they hope to challenge for a "Wembley double" of the FA Cup final and Division One play-off final.

20 February 2000 – Gillingham's FA Cup dreams are ended with a 5–0 defeat at Chelsea, while a 2–1 defeat at home to Aston Villa resigns Everton to another season of disappointment in [Walter Smith](/wiki/Walter_Smith "Walter Smith")'s second season as manager. Tranmere Rovers are eliminated by Newcastle United after a brave 3–2 defeat.

23 February 2000 – [Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews"), one of the greatest English footballers of all time, dies at the age of 85.

26 February 2000 – Wimbledon become the first team this season to be unbeaten twice in the league by Manchester United after holding them to a 2–2 draw at [Selhurst Park](/wiki/Selhurst_Park "Selhurst Park").

29 February 2000 – Manchester United have now extended their Premier League lead to six points as February ends, with Leeds United posing the closest threat, while Arsenal and Liverpool (along with a resurgent Chelsea) pose a more distant threat, and Sunderland are now focused on qualifying for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") rather than challenging for the league title. Sheffield Wednesday and Watford continue to prop up the Premier League, with Bradford City joining them in the drop zone.[[17]](#cite_note-19) Charlton Athletic and Manchester City continue to head the Division One promotion race, with Ipswich Town, Barnsley, Huddersfield Town and Birmingham City completing the top six.[[18]](#cite_note-20)

4 March 2000 – Debutant striker [Stan Collymore](/wiki/Stan_Collymore "Stan Collymore") scores a hat-trick for [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") in their 5–2 win over [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") in the Premier League, just days after his transfer from [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.").

7 March 2000 – [John Hartson](/wiki/John_Hartson "John Hartson")'s proposed move from [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") to [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") collapses after he fails a medical.

10 March 2000 – Liverpool pay a club record £11 million for Leicester City striker [Emile Heskey](/wiki/Emile_Heskey "Emile Heskey").

11 March 2000 – [Steffen Iversen](/wiki/Steffen_Iversen "Steffen Iversen") scores a hat-trick in Tottenham's 7–2 home league win over Southampton.

22 March 2000 – [Dean Windass](/wiki/Dean_Windass "Dean Windass") scores a hat-trick in Bradford City's 4–4 relegation crunch thriller with Derby County at [Valley Parade](/wiki/Valley_Parade "Valley Parade").

31 March 2000 – Manchester United are now looking all set for title glory as they head the table by a seven-point margin with Leeds United as their closest rivals. Liverpool, Chelsea and Arsenal are looking more and more distant a threat in the race to prevent the Premier League trophy from remaining at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"). At the other end of the table, Sheffield Wednesday and Watford are looking doomed, with Bradford City occupying the final relegation position and Derby County hovering dangerously close to the drop zone.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The race for Premier League football next season is headed by Charlton Athletic and Ipswich Town. Barnsley, Manchester City, Huddersfield Town and Birmingham City complete the top six.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

2 April 2000 – Aston Villa beat Bolton Wanderers on penalties after a goalless draw in the FA Cup semi-final at Wembley, to reach their first FA Cup final for 43 years.

5 April 2000 – Two Leeds United Fans, Chris Loftus and Kevin Speight are killed on the eve of the club's UEFA Cup Semi-final against Galatasaray.

9 April 2000 – Two goals from [Gus Poyet](/wiki/Gus_Poyet "Gus Poyet") give Chelsea a 2–1 win over Newcastle United in the FA Cup semi-final.

10 April 2000 – A late surge by Middlesbrough is unable to prevent Manchester United from winning a thrilling Premier League 4–3 at the [Riverside Stadium](/wiki/Riverside_Stadium "Riverside Stadium").

15 April 2000 – All Premier League matches kicked off at 3.06 pm, to commemorate the 96 Liverpool supporters who lost their lives in the [Hillsborough disaster](/wiki/Hillsborough_disaster "Hillsborough disaster").[[21]](#cite_note-23)

19 April 2000 – Derby County bring [Georgi Kinkladze](/wiki/Georgi_Kinkladze "Georgi Kinkladze") back to England after two years with [Ajax](/wiki/AFC_Ajax "AFC Ajax") after agreeing a £3 million deal. Manchester United surrender their defence of the [European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") after losing 3–2 at home to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") in the quarter-final second leg.

21 April 2000 – Charlton Athletic win promotion back to the Premier League after one season away.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

22 April 2000 – Manchester United seal their sixth Premier League title in eight seasons with a 3–1 win at Southampton.

29 April 2000 – Bradford City boost their survival bid and dent Wimbledon's with a 3–0 win at Valley Parade.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

30 April 2000 – April draws to a close with champions Manchester United an incredible 19 points ahead of second placed Arsenal, who have two games in hand. Liverpool are looking set for the third Champions League place, while Chelsea head the UEFA Cup race. Leeds United meanwhile have fallen to fifth place after a disastrous run of results, and now look unlikely to even qualify for European competition at all. Aston Villa, Sunderland and West Ham United remain in with a slim chance of taking the league's UEFA Cup slot.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Charlton Athletic have been confirmed as Division One champions, but the race for second place is still being contested by Manchester City and Ipswich Town.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

7 May 2000 – [Chester City](/wiki/Chester_City_F.C. "Chester City F.C.") are relegated to the [Football Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference") after 69 years of [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") membership. On the same day, Manchester City secure promotion to the Premier League as Division One runners-up (their second successive promotion), Walsall suffer relegation to Division Two, Burnley secure promotion to Division One as Division Two runners-up, and Northampton seal the third and final automatic promotion place in Division Three. Chester City's place in the Football League will be taken by Conference champions [Kidderminster Harriers](/wiki/Kidderminster_Harriers_F.C. "Kidderminster Harriers F.C.").

9 May 2000 – Sheffield Wednesday are relegated from the Premier League after nine successive seasons of top-flight football, but go down fighting with a 3–3 draw against Arsenal.

14 May 2000 – Wimbledon lose 2–0 at Southampton and are relegated to [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") after 14 years in the top flight, and 12 years to the day after their FA Cup triumph over Liverpool. Bradford City, meanwhile, confirm their survival with a 1–0 win over Liverpool, who surrender a Champions League place to Leeds United.

14 May 2000 – [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") announces a £500 million bid to bring live [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") matches onto terrestrial television for the first time. If the deal goes ahead, it will be the first time since 1992 (when the Premier League was created from the old [Football League First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division")) that live top division football has been shown on any terrestrial television channel.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

18 May 2000 – West Ham United sell [Marc-Vivien Foé](/wiki/Marc-Vivien_Fo%C3%A9 "Marc-Vivien Foé") to [Olympique Lyonnais](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais") for £6 million.

20 May 2000 – [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") beat [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") 1–0 in the last FA Cup final to be played at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") before the 77-year-old stadium is rebuilt. [Roberto Di Matteo](/wiki/Roberto_Di_Matteo "Roberto Di Matteo"), who scored for Chelsea in the opening minute of their 1997 triumph, scores the only goal of the game.

29 May 2000 – [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") seal promotion to the Premier League after a five-year exile by defeating [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") 4–2 in the Division One playoff final at Wembley. It is their first win at Wembley since they won the FA Cup in 1978.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

30 May 2000 – Arsenal sign [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") defender [Lauren](/wiki/Lauren_(Cameroonian_footballer) "Lauren (Cameroonian footballer)") for £7.2 million from [Mallorca](/wiki/RCD_Mallorca "RCD Mallorca").

31 May 2000 – Manchester United pay a national record fee for a goalkeeper when they sign [AS Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") and France goalkeeper [Fabien Barthez](/wiki/Fabien_Barthez "Fabien Barthez") for £7.8 million.

1 June 2000 – [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill") leaves [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") to take over as manager of [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C."). Tottenham Hotspur pay a club record £11 million for [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") striker [Serhii Rebrov](/wiki/Serhii_Rebrov "Serhii Rebrov").

2 June 2000 – Chelsea equal the national transfer record (set by [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer") four years ago) with a £15 million move for Jimmy Floyd Hasselbaink.

14 June 2000 – [Match of the Day](/wiki/Match_of_the_Day "Match of the Day"), the longest-running and most popular football programme on British television, is set to end after next season as a result of [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)")'s £183 million deal to show highlights from Premier League games.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

20 June 2000 – Chelsea sign [Icelandic](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") striker [Eiður Guðjohnsen](/wiki/Ei%C3%B0ur_Gu%C3%B0johnsen "Eiður Guðjohnsen") from Bolton Wanderers for £5 million.

## Famous debutants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Famous debutants")]

* [Ashley Cole](/wiki/Ashley_Cole "Ashley Cole"), 18, was involved in [Arsenal's](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") League Cup tie at [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") in November 1999, with his first taste of league action following in a loan spell at [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.").
* A future rival of Cole's for the England left-back slot, [Nicky Shorey](/wiki/Nicky_Shorey "Nicky Shorey"), 18, is brought on as substitute for [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient "Leyton Orient") at [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C.") in February 2000.
* [Shaun Wright-Phillips](/wiki/Shaun_Wright-Phillips "Shaun Wright-Phillips") helps [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") win at [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") in the League Cup in August 1999, two months before his 18th birthday.
* [Michael Carrick](/wiki/Michael_Carrick "Michael Carrick") makes his professional debut, for [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."), in the [Intertoto Cup](/wiki/Intertoto_Cup "Intertoto Cup") in the 1–1 draw with [FC Jokerit](/wiki/FC_Jokerit "FC Jokerit") in August 1999.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* 6 July 1999: [Johnny Campbell](/wiki/Johnny_Campbell_(footballer_born_1910) "Johnny Campbell (footballer born 1910)"), 89, who was born in [Ayrshire](/wiki/Ayrshire "Ayrshire"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), moved south of the border in 1931 to begin his professional career with [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."). He played 21 league games in attack over the next two years, scoring 12 goals, before transferring to [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") in 1933. He managed 104 goals in 184 league games before his professional career was ended by [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). He then continued his playing career at non-league level.
* 18 August 1999: [Alf Kirchen](/wiki/Alf_Kirchen "Alf Kirchen"), 85, was a prolific right-winger who started his career in style with 11 goals in 14 league games at [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") in the mid-1930s before he signed for [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."). He was then capped three times by England, scoring twice, and managed 38 goals in 92 league games for Arsenal before the outbreak of war. His career was ended by an injury suffered in a wartime match in 1943.
* 17 September 1999: [Fred Avey](/wiki/Fred_Avey "Fred Avey"), 90, had a promising start to his professional career with [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") in the late 1920s, scoring 28 goals in 62 league games as a forward, but lost his place in the team and was transferred to [Torquay United](/wiki/Torquay_United_F.C. "Torquay United F.C."), playing just three league games and dropping out of league football at the age of 24.
* 29 September 1999: [Walter Joyce](/wiki/Walter_Joyce "Walter Joyce"), 62, played 70 league games at wing-half for [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") between 1954 and 1964, later turning out for [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") and finally his hometown club [Oldham Athletic](/wiki/Oldham_Athletic_A.F.C. "Oldham Athletic A.F.C.") before retiring from playing in 1970. He was the father of fellow footballer [Warren Joyce](/wiki/Warren_Joyce "Warren Joyce"), who played 70 league games for Burnley between 1993 and 1996 and moved into management with [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C.") in November 1998.
* 16 October 1999: [Bill Dodgin senior](/wiki/Bill_Dodgin,_Sr. "Bill Dodgin, Sr."), 90, played at wing-half for [Huddersfield Town](/wiki/Huddersfield_Town_A.F.C. "Huddersfield Town A.F.C."), [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C."), [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C."), [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") and [Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") in the 1930s. He then managed [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C."), [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C."), [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") and then returned to Bristol Rovers in a managerial career which stretched from 1946 to 1972, with a 13-year interval between his departure from Brentford and appointment at Bristol Rovers. His son, [Bill junior](/wiki/Bill_Dodgin,_Jr. "Bill Dodgin, Jr."), played under him at Southampton and Fulham.
* 27 October 1999: [Johnny Byrne](/wiki/Johnny_Byrne_(footballer) "Johnny Byrne (footballer)"), 60, was a striker who played for [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C."), before commanding a [British transfer record fee](/wiki/British_football_transfer_record "British football transfer record") to go to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."), playing in the side that won the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") and the [Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/Cup_Winners%27_Cup "Cup Winners' Cup") in the mid-1960s. He also played for the [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") team, scoring 8 goals in 11 appearances, including a hattrick against a [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") side that featured [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio"). He later went back to Palace, then to [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C."), before rounding out his career in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), with Durban City. "Budgie" would spend the rest of his life there.
* 1 November 1999: [Dave Bickles](/wiki/Dave_Bickles "Dave Bickles"), 55, was a full-back in the same 1960s [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") side. He too later played for [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") and then [Colchester United](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.") before retiring from football to become a P.E. teacher in 1979. He was also a part-time coach for West Ham United.
* 23 December 1999:
  + – [Stan Flashman](/wiki/Stan_Flashman "Stan Flashman"), 69, rescued near-bankrupt non-league [Barnet](/wiki/Barnet_F.C. "Barnet F.C.") with a takeover deal in 1985. He made funds available to manager [Barry Fry](/wiki/Barry_Fry "Barry Fry") in hope of building a quality side, and this paid off with promotion to [the Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") in 1991, but he had quit within two years with the club knee deep in financial problems once again. Although Flashman's rash attitude (which saw him sack and reinstate Fry eight times) made him unpopular with most Barnet fans, it is unlikely that Barnet would have experienced league football without his takeover – they could well have gone out of business.
  + – [Billy McGlen](/wiki/Billy_McGlen "Billy McGlen"), 78, played 122 league games at wing-half for [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in the six seasons after the end of World War II. He later played for [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") and [Oldham Athletic](/wiki/Oldham_Athletic_A.F.C. "Oldham Athletic A.F.C.").
* 7 January 2000: [Ken Keyworth](/wiki/Ken_Keyworth "Ken Keyworth"), 65, scored 63 goals in 177 league games for [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") after joining them from his [hometown](/wiki/Rotherham "Rotherham") club [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C."), collecting a League Cup winner's medal at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street") in 1964, and scoring their consolation goal in the 1963 FA Cup final defeat to Manchester United. He was also on the losing side to Tottenham Hotspur in the 1961 final. He finished his playing career with brief spells at [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") and [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.").
* 30 January 2000: [Martin Aldridge](/wiki/Martin_Aldridge_(footballer) "Martin Aldridge (footballer)"), 25, [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") striker on loan to [Rushden & Diamonds](/wiki/Rushden_%26_Diamonds_F.C. "Rushden & Diamonds F.C."), died in an [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") hospital from injuries sustained in a car crash in [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire"). He had been a fairly consistent goalscorer for [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.") and [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") earlier in his career, and was a regular striker for Oxford during their 1996–97 Division Two promotion campaign.
* 23 February 2000: [Sir Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews"), 85, one of the greatest footballers England ever produced, died in his native [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") three weeks after his 85th birthday. Despite his on-the-field brilliance, he only ever won one major trophy – the 1953 [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") with [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."). That game was dubbed 'The Matthews Final' because he had so influenced his side's dramatic recovery from almost certain defeat at the hands of [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.").
* 14 April 2000: [Wilf Mannion](/wiki/Wilf_Mannion "Wilf Mannion"), 81, died just a few weeks after his old England teammate [Sir Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews"). Mannion had played 26 times for England in the immediate postwar years, and was a regular goalscorer for [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") at club level.
* 16 April 2000: [Harry Clarke](/wiki/Harry_Clarke_(footballer_born_1923) "Harry Clarke (footballer born 1923)"), 77, was ever-present in the title winning team of 1951 for Tottenham Hotspur, his only club. He played 295 league games at full-back for the [North Londoners](/wiki/North_London "North London") between 1949 and 1956, and won an England cap in 1954. After retiring as a player, he served the club as a coach.
* 9 June 2000: [Shay Brennan](/wiki/Shay_Brennan "Shay Brennan"), 63, made his [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") debut as a left-winger in February 1958 in the first game after the [Munich air disaster](/wiki/Munich_air_disaster "Munich air disaster"), the position left vacant by the death of [David Pegg](/wiki/David_Pegg "David Pegg") and injury to [Albert Scanlon](/wiki/Albert_Scanlon "Albert Scanlon"). He was soon converted to a left-back and remained at United until 1970, collecting two league title medals, an FA Cup winner's medal and a European Cup winner's medal as well as playing 355 league games. Despite being born in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), he qualified for play for the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") due to his ancestry and was capped 19 times. He later managed [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford_F.C. "Waterford F.C.") in the Irish leagues and lived in Ireland until his sudden death from a heart attack.
